title: - FINITE-TIME CONVERGENCE AND SAMPLE COM - PLEXITY OF MULTI-AGENT ACTOR-CRITIC REIN FORCEMENT LEARNING WITH AVERAGE REWARD [PDF]

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - FINITE-TIME CONVERGENCE AND SAMPLE COM - PLEXITY OF MULTI-AGENT ACTOR-CRITIC REIN FORCEMENT LEARNING WITH AVERAGE REWARD
Hairi and Jia Liu Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering The Ohio State University Columbus, OH 43210, USA hairi.1@osu.edu, liu@ece.osu.edu
Songtao Lu IBM Research AI IBM Thomas J. Watson Research Center Yorktown Heights, NY 10598 songtao@ibm.com

## ABSTRACT

In this paper, we establish the first finite-time convergence result of the actor-critic algorithm for fully decentralized multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) problems with average reward. In this problem, a set of N agents work cooperatively to maximize the global average reward through interacting with their neighbors over a communication network. We consider a practical MARL setting, where the rewards and actions of each agent are only known to itself, and the knowledge of joint actions of the agents is not assumed. Toward this end, we propose a mini-batch Markovian sampled fully decentralized actor-critic algorithm and analyze its finite-time convergence and sample complexity. We show that the sample complexity of this algorithm is O(N[2] /ǫ[2] log(N/ǫ)). Interestingly, this sample complexity bound matches that of the state-of-the-art single-agent actorcritic algorithms for reinforcement learning.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

1) Background and Motivations: In recent years, multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) has found a wide range of applications in networked large-scale systems, such as power grid systems (Riedmiller et al., 2000), autonomous driving (Yu et al., 2019; Shalev-Shwartz et al., 2016) and strategic games (Silver et al., 2018; Foerster et al., 2018), to name just a few. Although empirical successes of MARL applications have been widely observed, the fundamental theoretical understanding of how to develop fast-converging and low sample-complexity MARL algorithms, two of the most important performance metrics for MARL, remains in its infancy so far (see, e.g., (Zhang et al., 2021a) for an excellent survey). In particular, two important aspects of cooperative MARL algorithm designs deserve special attention:

- First, in the multi-agent collaborative setting, the information structure (i.e., the assumptions of who have the knowledge of what) is far more complex than its single-agent counterpart and care must be taken in MARL problem formulations. In the cooperative MARL literature so far, many existing works assume full knowledge of joint states and joint actions, which often do not hold true in practice. For example, in autonomous driving (Yu et al., 2019), each vehicle can only observe/detect the actions of the surrounding vehicles that are within its communication range. As another example, in power grid networks (Riedmiller et al., 2000), each power distributor generally does not know the resistor values set by other distributors.

- Second, most MARL theoretical studies in the MARL literature are focused on the discounted total reward setting, where a hyperparameter γ ∈ (0, 1) is introduced as the discount factor in the objective function. Although the discounted total reward setting captures the important aspect of diminishing return in the future, it may not be appropriate for many other applications where the long-term average reward is of interest. For example, in the optimization of distributed communication networks with MARL, the typical and natural performance metrics are long-term average throughput or latency in the steady-state.
The lack of a fundamental understanding on how to develop efficient cooperative MARL algorithms that consider the above two aspects in terms of information structure, scalability, and communication

1

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

and sample complexities motivates us to fill this gap by developing a fully decentralized cooperative MARL algorithm in the average reward setting, without assuming joint action knowledge.

2) Technical Challenges: Developing a fully decentralized cooperative MARL algorithm for the average reward setting without full joint action knowledge is highly non-trivial and several major technical challenges naturally arise. First, it is well-known that, even in the single-agent reinforcement learning (RL) setting, the average reward setting is more challenging to analyze compared to the discounted reward setting, which necessitates different proof techniques (Tsitsiklis & Van Roy, 1999). In MARL, the decentralized nature and the lack of joint action-state information further complicate the algorithm design and analysis in the average reward setting. Second, due to the lack of joint action knowledge, the communication costs among agents in MARL will be significantly increased to achieve a satisfying performance, which implies that low sample and communication complexities are even more challenging and critical for MARL without joint action information.

3) Main Results and Contributions: The main contribution of this paper is that we overcome the aforementioned challenges and develop a consensus-based Markovian sampled decentralized actorcritic algorithm for MARL. Our key results in this paper are summarized as follows:

- We propose a batch-sampled actor-critic algorithm that uses consensus updates in TD-sharing among agents. This batch sampling approach enables more efficient communication compared to the classical fully decentralized MARL. Specifically, in order to converge to an ǫ neighborhood of the stationary, we require O(ǫ[−][1] log(ǫ[−][1] )) rounds of communication while only needing O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )) samples. By contrast, the state-of-the-art MARL requires a communication round per sampling. Also, our algorithm allows the use of constant step-sizes in both the actor and the critic steps.

- We provide the first-ever sample complexity analysis in the MARL average reward problem setting without joint action information. Our obtained complexity is O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )), where ǫ is the closeness to the neighborhood of stationary point (treating network size N as a fixed constant). It is worth noting that the order-wise sample complexity of our algorithm matches that of the state-of-the-art single-agent RL algorithms.

## 2 RELATED WORK

In this section, we provide a quick overview on the closely related work on MARL algorithms and their theoretical results, along with several notable related counterparts in single-agent RL.

1) MARL Theoretical Analysis and Algorithm Design: For recent advances in MARL algorithms and their theoretical results, Zhang et al. (2021a) provided a comprehensive survey. Also, Lee et al. (2020) highlighted the evolution from single-agent to multi-agent RL from a distributed optimization perspective. In the broader area of MARL, a line of research has been focused on the MARL policy evaluation problem. These works analyzed the convergence Doan et al. (2019) and proposed various variance reduction of policy evaluation in decentralized MARL algorithms Zhang et al. (2021b). Doan et al. (2019) used i.i.d. sampling and has shown the sample complexity of O[˜] (ǫ[−][1] ) for their TD(0) learning algorithm to reach a mean-square error convergence. However, these algorithms do not involve policy improvement and solely focus on the performance evaluation of given policies. In the areas of joint policy evaluation and improvement, Foerster et al. (2018) considered multi-agent actor-critic algorithm that has a centralized critic and decentralized actors, which is different from our fully decentralized actor-critic algorithm. In contrast, Zhang et al. (2018) established asymptotic convergence results for fully decentralized MARL actor-critic algorithms. Concurrent with our work, Chen et al. (2021) has recently studied the mini-batch Markovian sampling actor-critic algorithm for a class of discounted reward MARL problems, where the finite-time convergence result is obtained. They have applied batch sampling for both actor and critic steps and achieved a sample complexity of O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )), which is the same as ours. We note that, together with our work, these are the first finite-time convergence results for MARL. However, there are several key differences between our work and (Chen et al., 2021). First, we focus on the average reward problem, while Chen et al. (2021) studied the discounted reward setting. Second, in (Chen et al., 2021), agents share a noisy version of the rewards with the neighbors, which requires a re-sampling process from every sampled reward instance. In contrast, we allow agents to share local TD-errors with their neighbors and no re-sampling is required.

2) Related Literature in Single-Agent RL: We note that single-agent RL can be viewed as a centralized approach, where a central controller collects joint actions, rewards and even designs policies for agents. For the single-agent average reward setting, Tsitsiklis & Van Roy (1999) first analyzed the asymptotic convergence of TD(λ) algorithm with function approximations in the policy evaluation problem. Also, Tsitsiklis & Van Roy (2002) provided insights in terms of differences and connections between average reward and discounted reward of TD-based learning algorithms with function approximations. Recently, Qiu et al. (2021) analyzed the sample complexity for an actorcritic algorithm for the average reward problem. In their actor-critic algorithm, they used batch sampling for the critic learning and i.i.d. sampling for the actor step, with sample complexity being O(ǫ[−][3] log[2] (ǫ[−][1] )). By applying mini-batch sampling update, we are able to improve the sample complexity by a factor of O(ǫ[−][1] ). Another closely related work on single-agent RL is (Xu et al., 2020), where the authors studied the discounted reward problem. They used batch sampling for both actor and critic steps in their actor-critic algorithm and developed a new technique to handle bias error in the critic step, which we also adopted for the average approximation parameter analysis in our critic step. This achieved the state-of-the-art sample complexity of O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )) for singleagent RL. In addition, the global convergence of actor-critic algorithm to the optimal policy has been studied in the case of discounted setting with single time scale in (Fu et al., 2020) and linear quadratic regulator in (Yang et al., 2019). However, we note that these settings are fundamentally different from the average reward setting and it will be an interesting future direction to consider global convergence possibility in the average reward setting.
To conclude this section, we summarize the aforementioned related actor-critic and TD algorithms and their sample complexity results in Table 1.

## 3 MULTI-AGENT REINFORCEMENT LEARNING WITH AVERAGE REWARD

## 3.1 SYSTEM MODEL

Consider a multi-agent system with N agents, denoted by N = {1, · · · , N }, operating in a networked environment. Let E be the edge set for a given network G = (N , E). To formulate our MARL problem and facilitate our subsequent discussions, we first define the notion of networked multi-agent MDP as follows.

Definition 1 (Networked Multi-Agent MDP). Let G = (N , E) be a communication network that connects N agents. A networked multi-agent MDP is defined by following tuple (S, {A[i] }i∈N , P, {R[i] }i∈N , G), where S is the global state space observed by all agents, A[i] is the action set for agent i, and P : S ×A×S → [0, 1] is a global state transition function, and R[i] : S ×A is the local reward function for agent i. Let A =[�] i∈N[A][i][ be the joint action set of all agents.]

In this paper, we assume that the global state space S and action space for agent A[i] are finite. As a result, the joint action space A is also finite for finite N . We also note that at time t ≥ 0, all agents can observe the current global state st. However, agent i can only observe its own action a[i] t[∈A][i][, which is the key difference between our model and that in (Zhang et al., 2018), where it is] assumed that the joint actions are observable to all agents. Moreover, each agent can only observe its own reward rt[i][,][i.e.,][agents][do][not][observe][or][share][rewards][with][other][agents][at][time][t][.][The]

reward function R[i] (s, a) is an expectation given s and a, and the instantaneous reward is denoted by r[i] (s, a), i.e., R[i] (s, a) = E[r[i] (s, a)].

We consider policies that are stationary. In our MARL system, each agent chooses its action following its local policy π[i] that is conditioned on the current global state s, i.e., π[i] (a[i] |s) is the probability for agent i to choose an action a[i] ∈A[i] . Then, the joint policy π : S × A → [0, 1] can be written as π(a|s) =[�] i∈N[π][i][(][a][i][|][s][)][.]

Moreover, the policies at the agents are parameterized. Specifically, each agent i’s local policy can be written as π[i][where][θ][i][∈][R][m][i][denotes][the][parameter.][We][let][θ][≜][[(][θ][1][)][T][ ,][ · · ·][,][ (][θ][N][)][T][ ]][T][∈] θ[i][,] R�Ni=1[m][i] . Then, we can write the joint policy as follows: πθ(a|s) =[�] i∈N[π][θ][i][(][a][i][|][s][)][.]

## 3.2 TECHNICAL ASSUMPTIONS

We now state the following assumptions on the positivity and continuity of πθ[i][i][(][a][i][|][s][)][,][which guar-] antee the stationary distribution of {st} under any given policy. Assumption 1. For any i ∈N , s ∈S, a[i] ∈A[i] and θ[i] ∈ R[m][i] , the policy function πθ[i][i][(][a][i][|][s][)][≥][0][.] Also, πθ[i][i][(][s, a][)][is][a][continuously][differentiable][with][respect][to][the][parameter][θ][i][.][In][addition,][for] any θ, we assume the induced Markov chain {st}t≥0 is irreducible and aperiodic, and its transition matrix P[θ] is P[θ] (s[′] |s) =[�] a∈A[π][θ][(][a][|][s][)][ ·][ P][(][s][′][|][s, a][)][,] ∀s, s[′] ∈S.

Assumption 1 guarantees that the states have a stationary distribution dθ(s) over S given any policy πθ. As a result, the Markov chain of state action pair {(st, at)} also has a stationary distribution dθ(s) · πθ(a|s).
Assumption 2. The instantaneous reward rt[i][is uniformly bounded by a constant][ r][max][>][0][ for any] i ∈N and t ≥ 0.

Assumption 2 is common in the literature (see, e.g., (Zhang et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2020; Doan et al., 2019)) and easy to be satisfied in many practical MDP models with finite state and action spaces.

Assumption 3. Let A be a consensus weight matrix for a given communication network G. There exists a positive constant η > 0 such that A ∈ R[N][×][N] is doubly stochastic and Aii ≥ η, ∀i ∈N . Moreover, Aij ≥ η if i, j are connected, otherwise Aij = 0 for all i, j.

Assumption 3 is standard in the distributed multi-agent optimization literature Nedic & Ozdaglar (2009). We remark that for a practical choice of A, one can use the following form A = 1[−][L][)][,][where][deg][(][G][)][is][the][degree][of][the][graph][G][(i.e.][the][maximal][vertex][de-] deg(G)[(][deg][(][G][)][ ·][ I] gree), I is the identity matrix of conforming dimensionality, and L is the Laplacian matrix of the graph. It is easy to verify that this matrix is symmetric, doubly stochastic and η ≥ deg1(G)[≥] N[1][.]

Assumption 4. Each agent i’s value function is parameterized by the class of linear functions, i.e., Vθ(s; w) = φ(s)[T] w where φ(s) ≜ [φ1(s), · · · , φK (s)][T] ∈ R[K] is the feature associated with the state s ∈S and K < |S|. The feature vectors φ(s) are uniformly bounded for any s ∈S. Without loss of generality, we assume that ∥φ(s)∥≤ 1. Furthermore, the feature matrix Φ ∈ R[|S|×][K] has full column rank. Also, for any u ∈ R[K] , Φu ̸= 1, where 1 is an all-one vector.

This assumption on features is standard and has been widely adopted in the literature, e.g., (Tsitsiklis & Van Roy, 1999; Zhang et al., 2018; Qiu et al., 2021). This assumption implies the following property: for any policy πθ, the inequality w[T] Aπθ w < 0 holds for any w = 0, where Aπθ is

This property further implies that for all θ, Aπθ is invertible and λmax(Aπθ + A[T] πθ[)][ <][ 0][ (Qiu et al.,] 2021), where λmax(·) is the largest eigenvalues of the matrix. Assumption 5. There exists a constant λA > 0 such that λmax(Aπθ + A[T] πθ[)][≤−][λ][A][holds for all] θ ∈ R�i∈N[m][i] .
Assumption 5 ensures the optimal approximation wθ[∗][for any given policy][ π][θ][is uniformly bounded] (see discussion before Theorem 2 and Lemma 4).

Assumption 6. Let ψθ(s, a) = ∇θ log πθ(a|s) be the score function for any state-action pair (s, a). For any two policy parameters θ, θ[′] ∈ R�i∈N[m][i] , and any state-action pair (s, a) ∈S×A, there exist positive constants such that the following hold: 1): ∥ψθ(s, a)∥≤ Cψ; 2): ∥∇θJ(θ) −∇θJ(θ[′] )∥≤ LJ ∥θ − θ[′] ∥; where J(θ) is defined in (2) and ∥· ∥ denotes the ℓ2-norm.

Assumption 6 says that the score function is uniformly bounded for any policy and the gradient of the objective function has a Lipschitz property with respect to the policy parameter. This assumption has also been adopted in the analysis of the single-agent actor-critic algorithm in (Qiu et al., 2021). We note that for the discounted reward problem, this gradient Lipschitz property can be guaranteed through (Xu et al., 2020, Assumption 2). We note that Assumption 6 can be satisfied by the class of soft-max policy under the Assumption 1, as in (Guo et al., 2021).

## 3.4 POLICY GRADIENT THEOREM

The gradient of a policy πθ for decentralized policy gradient is stated in the following theorem. Theorem 1 (Policy Gradient Theorem for MARL (Zhang et al., 2018)). For any θ, let πθ : S ×A → [0, 1] be a policy and let J(θ) be the global average long-term average return defined in (2). Then, the gradient of J(θ) with respect to parameter θ[i] can be computed as:

## 4 A CONSENSUS-BASED ACTOR-CRITIC ALGORITHM

In this section, we propose a consensus-based actor-critic algorithm that includes two key steps: actor and critic. In the critic step, the algorithm evaluates the value functions for the policy πθt at time t. After the critic step, the algorithm enters the actor step, which improves the policy parameter θt according to the direction from policy gradient as shown in Theorem 1. In both steps, we use constant step-sizes and adopt batch sampling.

In this paper, we use linear function approximations for the value functions. Specifically, each agent i has a parameter w[i] ∈ R[K] to approximate the global value functions Vθ(s; w[i] ) for each state s ∈S. For linear approximation, we have Vθ(s; w[i] ) = φ(s)[T] w[i] , where φ(s) ∈ R[K] denotes the feature for state s. As a result, the gradient of value function at state s with respect to approximation parameter w[i] is φ(s), i.e. ∇wVθ(s; w[i] ) = φ(s).

1) The Critic Step: The critic step is achieved through its own oracle, which is summarized in Algorithm 1. In the critic step, we allow the agents to communicate the approximation parameters w(s) with their neighbors via the communication network with consensus weight matrix A. For agent i ∈N , the parameter is locally updated by following rules:

where β > 0 is the step-size of the critic step, µ[i] k,τ[is][the][estimate][of][the][long-term][return][of] agent i, and δk,τ[i][is][the][local][TD-error][for][agent][i][at][iteration][k][using][sample][τ][.][Here,][in][each] iteration k in the critic step, the approximation parameter is locally updated through a batch of sampling as in (7), where the batch size is M . Then, agent i will further update the approximation parameter w[i] through a weighted average of its local and neighboring agents’ parameters as follows: wk[i] +1[=][ �] j∈Ni[A][(][i, j][) ˜][w] k[i][.][ This batched sampling update continues for][ K][c][ iterations for each given] policy πθt.

2) The Actor Step: As shown in Theorem 1, the advantage function needs to be known to compute the gradient. However, from the definition in (3), the joint action a also has to be known to compute the advantage function, whereas in our model, each agent can only observe its own action. As a result, an estimation of the advantage function is required. Here, we show that the global TD-error is an unbiased estimate of the advantage function.

At time t, suppose we have samples st, at, st+1 and the rewards {rt[i] +1[}][i][∈N][then][the][advantage] function is as follows: Advθ(st, at) = E[¯rt+1¯ − J(θ) + Vθ(St+1) − Vθ(st)|st, at], and the global TD-error can be computed as follows: δt = rt+1 − µt + Vθ(st+1) − Vθ(st), where µt+1 = (1 − α) · µt + α · ¯rt+1 is the estimate for the average long term return, and α is the step-size for the actor step. Hence, we have that the expected global TD-error is the advantage function, i.e., E[δt|st, at] = Aθ(st, at). Thus, we can use this global TD-error as an unbiased estimate of the advantage function. For agent i, the local TD-error can be computed as δt[i][=][r] t[i] +1[−][µ][i] t[+][ V][θ][(][s][t][+1][)][ −][V][θ][(][s][t][)][,][where] µ[i] t+1[= (1][ −][α][)][µ][i] t[+][ αr] t[i] +1[.][We also note that][ µ][t][=] N[1] �i∈N[µ] t[i][.]

Thus, once each agent knows the global TD-error, the policy parameter can be updated according to the policy gradient rule in (4). However, without any communication, each agent only has the knowledge of its own local TD-error. Moreover, we will show that the networked TD-error is actually the average of the local TD-errors. Specifically,

For any time t, the average of the local TD-errors is an unbiased estimate of the advantage function. Therefore, we just need to let each agent communicate with its neighbors so that an average of all local TD-errors can be reached or estimated for all agents.

From the results in (Nedic & Ozdaglar, 2009), we have limτ →∞ A[τ] (x[1] , · · · , x[N] )[T] = N1 �i∈N[x][i][1][.][However,][this][convergence][is][asymptotic,][meaning][that][the][exact][estimation][can] only be achieved with infinite iterations (i.e., τ →∞). In practice, since one can only apply finite iterations, we use δ[˜] t[i][to denote the estimate of the global TD-error maintained by agent][ i][ after] tgossip iterations of updates at time t, i.e., δ[˜] t[i][=][[][A][t][gossip][]][i][∆][0] t[,][where][ ∆][0] t[=][(][δ] t[1][,][ · · ·][, δ] t[N][)][T][is the][ N][-] dimension vector of local TD-errors at time t. We note that agents do not need to know the weight information of other agents. Rather, each agent just needs to exchange updated estimate of the local TD-errors with its neighbors for tgossip rounds as shown in Lines 11-16 of Algorithm 2. This communication among agents is also done in a batch fashion, with batch size being B. This implies that for each outer iteration t ∈{0 · · · , T − 1}, only tgossip rounds of communication for every B samples are needed.

Combined with a B-batched Markovian sampling, the parameter θ[i] update for agent i ∈N can be written as follows: θt[i] +1[=][θ] t[i][+] B[α] �Bl=0−1[δ][˜] t,l[i][·][ ψ] t,l[i][where][ α][ is the step-size for the actor step and] ψt,l[i][=][∇][θ][i][ log][ π] θ[i] t[i][(][s][t,l][, a] t,l[i][)][ is the local score function for agent][ i][ using][ l][-th sample at time][ t][.][The] actor step of our algorithm is illustrated in Algorithm 2.

## 5 THEORETICAL CONVERGENCE ANALYSIS

In this section, we present the convergence results for both the critic and actor steps in Theorems 2 and 3, respectively. Due to space limitation, we relegate the proofs to the supplementary material.

## 5.1 CONVERGENCE ANALYSIS FOR CRITIC (ALGORITHM 1)

¯ For a given policy πθ, we define bπθ = Es∼dθ,a∼πθ [φ(s)(¯r(s, a) − J(θ))], where r(s, a) = N1 �i∈N[r][i][(][s, a][)][and][J][(][θ][)][are][as][defined][in][(2).][For][all][agents,][the][optimal][solution][(Wu et al.,] 2020), (Qiu et al., 2021) of this critic learning is wθ[∗][=][−][A][−] πθ[1][b][π] θ[, where][ A][π] θ[is defined as in (1).] The invertiblity of Aπθ is due to the Assumptions 1 and 4 (see more details in (Qiu et al., 2021; Tsitsiklis & Van Roy, 1999)). Then, the convergence of the critic step is summarized as follows: Theorem 2 (Convergence and Sample Complexity of the Critic). Suppose that Assumptions 1-5 hold. For any given policy πθ, consider the iteration generated by Algorithm 1. Recall the definition of λA in the Assumption 5 and let β < min{ 2(1η−[N] η[−][N][1][−][1] )[,] 128[λ][A][,] λ4A[}][.][It then follows that:]

Theorem 2 establishes a convergence result for the policy evaluation of a given policy πθ. We can see˜ that our constant step-size batch-sampled critic process can achieve the same complexity of O(ǫ[−][1] ) order-wise as the TD(0) learning in Doan et al. (2019), where diminishing step-sizes were used. On the other hand, in the single agent average reward setting of Qiu et al. (2021), there exists a non-vanishing error term in their critic convergence. In contrast, with proper choices of iteration number Kc and batch size M , our mean-square error can be arbitrarily small.

## 5.2 CONVERGENCE ANALYSIS FOR ACTOR-CRITIC ALGORITHM (ALGORITHM 2)

Define the approximation error introduced by using linear approximation in the critic step, ξapprox[critic][=] maxθ∈R�i∈N[mi][E][s][∼][d][θ][[][|][V][π][θ][(][s][)][ −][V][w] πθ[∗][(][s][)][|][2][]][.][For][a][given][policy][π][θ][,][this][error][represents][the][gap] between ground truth value function under such policy and the value function obtained by the best possible linear approximation. Such an error term is standard in the literature where linear approximations are adopted (Qiu et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2020). Let Rw, defined in Lemma 4, be an upper bound on ||wθ|| for all policy parameter θ.
Theorem 3 (Overall Convergence Rate and Sample Complexity). Suppose that Assumption 1-6 1 hold. Consider the actor-critic algorithm in Algorithm 2. Let step-size α = 4LJ[.][It then holds that:]

where κ3 is a positive constant. Furthermore, let T ≥[64][L][J] ǫ[r][max] , B ≥ 576[(][r][max][+][2][R][w] (1[)] −[2][[] ρ[1] )[+(][2][κ][−][1][)][ρ][]] Nǫ and the communication round among the agents tgossip ≥[1] 2[log][(1][−][η][N][−][1][)][−][1][64][κ] ǫ[3][N][ 3] . Suppose for the same setting as in Theorem 1 holds so that E[[�][N] i=1[||][w] K[i] c[−][w] θ[∗][||][2][]][≤] 288ǫ N[for][all][0][≤][t][≤] T , then we have E[||∇θJ(θt)||[2] ] ≤ ǫ + O(ξapprox[critic][)][,][with][a][total][complexity][of][(][B][+][ MK][c][)][T][=] O([N] ǫ[2][ 2][log(][N] ǫ[))][.][And the communication complexity is][ (][K][c][ +][ t][gossip][)][T][=][ O][(][1] ǫ[log(][N] ǫ[))][.]

8

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

**----- Start of picture text -----**

(a) System size N = 6. (b) System size N = 10. (c) System size N = 15.
**----- End of picture text -----**

Figure 1: Our TD-sharing algorithm vs classical MARL algorithm.

Theorem 3 concludes the overall sample complexity of our proposed actor-critic algorithm. The sample complexity of O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )) matches the state-of-the-art single-agent actor-critic RL by Xu et al. (2020) and the discounted MARL by Chen et al. (2021).

We note that the overall communication complexity also matches that of (Chen et al., 2021) in the discounted reward setting. However, our work is still an improvement compared to the classical MARL in Zhang et al. (2018) for the average reward setting. Specifically, Zhang et al. (2018) needed a communication round after each sampling. By contrast, in this paper, we only need a communication round per O(ǫ[−][1] ) sampling. This is thanks to the use of batch sampling in the actor step. Also because of the batch sampling, we are able to use a constant step-size for both actor and critic steps. Here, the overall communication cost is measured by the number of communication rounds rather than the size of bits transmitted over the network. We follow the standard definition of communication complexity in the literature, which is widely adopted in the literature, see (Chen et al., 2018) (Zhang et al., 2019). However, we note that tgossip, actor step batch size B and critic iteration rounds Kc, scale with O(log 1/ǫ), O(1/ǫ) and O(log 1/ǫ) respectively as indicated in the Theorem 3 and 2. The amount of information (in terms of bits) is (KcNK + BNtgossip)T = O( ǫ[N][2][log][N] ǫ[)][.]

## 6 EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS

In this section, we conduct experiments to compare our proposed consensus-based TD-sharing MARL algorithm with the most related MARL algorithm 1 in Zhang et al. (2018) that also studied average reward. To our knowledge, this is the only work that is directly comparable to ours. The key difference is that the knowledge of joint action is assumed in Zhang et al. (2018), but not in our work. We vary the system size from N = 6, 10 to 15. The blue curve is our TD-sharing algorithm and the red curve is classical MARL algorithm in Zhang et al. (2018). The curves represent the average results of 10 trials and the 95% confidence intervals are also plotted. For the details, see A.1 in Appendix. The results in Figure 1 show that for different system sizes, both algorithms converge to a reasonable objective value. Note that if we use uniformly random policy as the baseline policy, then the objective values will be around 2 due to the setting of our experiments. All simulation results are above this threshold and our TD-sharing algorithm converge to a better objective value. See Section A.2 for addition experiment results.

## 7 CONCLUSION AND DISCUSSION

In this paper, we studied fully decentralized MARL in average reward setting and proposed a batch-sampled actor-critic algorithm. Our main contribution is to establish the first finite time convergence result for fully decentralized MARL in average reward setting, where the complexity is O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )), which matches that of the state-of-the-art single agent RL. The algorithm reaches such convergence with a better communication efficiency. However, it is still in the preliminary stage of the convergence analysis of the MARL since we only used the vanilla average. The future direction will be how to design a more scalable algorithm in terms of system size.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents the first finite-time convergence analysis for decentralized multi-agent actor-critic reinforcement learning in the average reward setting. The work is theoretically ambitious and addresses a significant gap in the literature regarding information structure (no joint action knowledge) and reward formulation. However, there are several localized technical inconsistencies in the formulas and parameter definitions, particularly regarding the scaling of terms like the feature matrix property and sample complexity constants, which require careful reconciliation.

comments.title: Scaling inconsistency in feature matrix property
comments.quote: inequality w^{T} A_{\pi_{\theta}} w < 0 holds for any w = 0, where A_{\pi_{\theta}} is
comments.explanation: In the text following Assumption 4, the authors state that $w^T A_{\pi_\theta} w < 0$ for any $w$. However, the definition of $A_{\pi_\theta}$ is not explicitly provided in the main text (it is cut off or missing in the extraction). In standard literature (Tsitsiklis & Van Roy, 1999), this matrix involves the stationary distribution. More importantly, Assumption 5 defines $\lambda_{A}$ based on the maximum eigenvalue: $\lambda_{max}(A_{\pi_{\theta}} + A_{\pi_{\theta}}^T) \leq -\lambda_A$. Since the matrix is negative definite, the maximum eigenvalue is negative. However, the subsequent use of $\lambda_A$ in step-size constraints (e.g., $\lambda_A^4$) and convergence bounds suggests the authors are treating $\lambda_A$ as a positive constant representing the absolute value of the spectral gap. This is a minor notation flip but could confuse a reader checking the sign of the step-size $\beta$.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency in sample complexity scaling
comments.quote: sample complexity of this algorithm is O(N^{2} /\epsilon^{2} \log(N/\epsilon))
comments.explanation: In the Abstract, the sample complexity is stated to scale with $N^2$. However, in the Conclusion (Section 7) and Theorem 3, the dependence is somewhat ambiguous. Generally, in decentralized consensus-based algorithms, the $N$ factor enters through the network topology (spectral gap of $A$). Theorem 3 states a complexity of $O(\frac{N^2}{\epsilon^2} \log \frac{N}{\epsilon})$, but the text in Section 3.3 and the logic in Algorithm 2 suggest that $B$ and $M$ (batch sizes) scale with $N$. I checked if the $N^2$ might be $N$ (from rewards) times $N$ (from network consensus), which is plausible, but the paper does not explicitly derive the transition from the $\kappa$ constants to the $N^2$ factor in the theorem statements.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the definition of the network consensus matrix A
comments.quote: A_{ij} \geq \eta if i, j are connected, otherwise A_{ij} = 0 for all i, j.
comments.explanation: Assumption 3 requires $A$ to be doubly stochastic and have entries $\geq \eta$ for neighbors. Later, the authors suggest a construction $A = I - \frac{L}{deg(G)}$. For this to be doubly stochastic and satisfy the property, the graph must be regular or the scaling must be handled differently (e.g., Metropolis-Hastings weights). Specifically, if $A = I - L/d_{max}$, then $A_{ii}$ is $1 - d_i/d_{max}$. For a node with max degree, $A_{ii}$ could be 0, violating $A_{ii} \geq \eta$. While this is a standard construction, the specific constraints on $\eta$ and the construction provided are slightly at odds unless the graph is $d$-regular.

comments.title: Inconsistency in TD-error notation
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1 (Line 8), the TD-error for the critic uses the local parameter $w_k^i$. In Algorithm 2 (Line 8), a similar update is performed. However, the theoretical goal is to estimate the global average value function. The convergence in Theorem 2 suggests $w_k^i$ converges to $w^*$. However, in Algorithm 2, Line 20 uses a consensus-averaged TD-error $\tilde{\delta}$ to update the actor. There is a potential mismatch: the critic evaluates a value function $V$ that is intended to be the average reward value function, but each agent maintains local estimates $\mu^i$ of its local reward return (Algorithm 1, Line 7). For the actor to move in the direction of the *global* gradient, $\delta$ must reflect the global reward. The authors mention in Section 4.2 that 'networked TD-error is actually the average of the local TD-errors'. This justifies why the sum of local TD-errors (using local rewards) equals the global TD-error. However, this assumes that $w_t^i$ has reached consensus. If $w_t^i$ are different, the sum of terms $\phi(s)^T w_t^i$ does not represent the global value function. The paper assumes the consensus error in $w$ is small enough (from Theorem 2), but the explicit coupling in Algorithm 2 could be better justified.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper presents a well-structured and technically sound contribution to decentralized multi-agent reinforcement learning, establishing the first finite-time convergence result for actor-critic algorithms in the average reward setting without joint action knowledge. The theoretical framework is built upon solid assumptions, and the proposed minibatch consensus-based algorithm demonstrates competitive sample complexity compared to state-of-the-art single-agent and MARL methods. The clarity of the algorithmic descriptions and convergence analysis is strong, supported by meaningful comparisons in Table 1 and experimental validation. However, there are several critical inconsistencies in notation, formula scaling, and parameter dependencies—particularly concerning dimensional factors of N—that affect the correctness of the stated sample complexity bounds and undermine confidence in the tightness of the analysis. These issues are primarily quantitative but have implications for the scalability claims made in the paper.

comments.title: Incorrect sample complexity due to inconsistent N² scaling
comments.explanation: The abstract and Table 1 state the sample complexity as O(N²/ε² log(N/ε)), while Theorem 3 in Section 5.2 claims the total sample complexity is O(N²/ε² log(N/ε)) in the bound on E[||∇θJ(θt)||²]. However, the actual derivation in the cited supplementary material (not shown) must account for how constants scale with N. In Theorem 2, the condition includes terms like ||w̄₀ − wθ*||² scaled by N, and the error bound requires the mean squared error across agents to be O(ε/N). Combined with the batch size M = O(N/ε), iteration count Kc = O(log(N/ε)), and T = O(1/ε), the total sample complexity becomes (M Kc + B) T = O((N/ε * log(N/ε) + 1/ε) * 1/ε) = O(N log(N/ε)/ε² + 1/ε²), which is not matching the claimed O(N²/ε² log(N/ε)). The N² factor appears inflated. The authors likely intended to reflect consensus error scaling with network size, but as written, the bound is inconsistent with the parameters used in the analysis. This impacts the main claim about matching single-agent complexity.

comments.title: Mismatch between text and equation for critic sample complexity
comments.quote: then we have E[Ni=1||w Kic−wθ∗||2]≤ǫ [...] with total sample complexity given by KcM = O(N ǫ log(N ǫ))
comments.explanation: Theorem 2 states that the sum of squared errors over N agents is bounded by ε, which implies each agent's error is on average ε/N. But the total sample complexity is cited as KcM = O(N / ε log(N / ε)). From the conditions, M ≥ 6κ₄N / ε and Kc ≥ O(log(1/γ) · log(N / ε)) with γ < 1. This gives KcM = O((N / ε) log(N / ε)), which matches the O(N / ε log(N / ε)) form. However, this contradicts the standard interpretation where sample complexity should decrease as ε decreases—the denominator should be ε, not ǫ in numerator. Likely a typo in the complexity expression: 'O(N ǫ log(N ǫ))' should be 'O(N / ǫ log(N / ǫ))' to reflect inverse dependence on ǫ. This aligns with typical TD learning bounds and would resolve the inconsistency.

comments.title: Inconsistent handling of average reward in TD-error definition
comments.explanation: The local TD-error in Algorithm 1 uses µ[i]_k,τ, which is a per-agent estimate of the long-term average reward. However, the global average reward J(θ) is defined as (1/N)∑ᵢ Rⁱ(s,a), and the correct TD-error for average reward should subtract a consistent estimate of J(θ), not agent-specific µ[i]. The text later defines µ_t = (1/N)∑ᵢ µ[i]_t, suggesting that the consensus average is used. But in Algorithm 1, each agent uses its own µ[i], which may not converge to the same value without consensus on µ. This creates a mismatch: the theoretical analysis assumes a global J(θ) estimate, but the algorithm uses decentralized µ[i]. Either the µ[i] should be consensus-averaged before use, or the analysis should account for bias from inconsistent baselines. This affects the unbiasedness of the gradient estimate.

comments.title: Ambiguous subscript handling in consensus update
comments.explanation: The consensus update in Algorithm 1 writes w̃k[j] with j as neighbor index, but w̃k[i] was defined only for agent i in the local update. The notation suggests that each agent j computes its own w̃k[j], which is correct, but the indexing in the sum should be clear that w̃k[j] refers to the updated parameter of neighbor j. The current notation is understandable in context, but risks confusion if A(i,j) is interpreted as accessing a matrix of vectors. Standard notation would use wk+1[i] ← ∑ⱼ A(i,j) w̃k[j], with w̃k[j] being the post-update value of agent j. This is minor, as the surrounding text clarifies the peer-to-peer exchange, but could mislead a reader unfamiliar with consensus algorithms.

comments.title: Overstated claim about communication complexity
comments.quote: we require O(ǫ[−][1] log(ǫ[−][1])) rounds of communication while only needing O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1])) samples
comments.explanation: In Section 1, the authors claim O(1/ε) communication rounds, but Theorem 3 states tgossip = O(log(N/ε)) per outer iteration and T = O(1/ε), leading to total communication rounds (tgossip)T = O((log(N/ε))/ε), which is O((log(1/ε))/ε), not O(1/ε). The claim omits the logarithmic factor, which is non-negligible in finite-time analysis. While log(1/ε) grows slowly, omitting it overstates the efficiency. The claim might refer to order up to log factors, but in a paper emphasizing 'finite-time' and sample complexity, such precision matters. A careful reader could reasonably conclude the communication scaling is strictly 1/ε, which is incorrect.

comments.title: Underspecified initialization of µ[i] in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: Input : s0, πθt , φ, step-size β, critic step iteration number K, critic batch size M , the communication network A
comments.explanation: The algorithm initializes sk,0 but does not specify the initial value of µ[i]_k,τ. Since µ[i] is updated recursively via exponential averaging, its initialization affects early estimates of the average reward. The convergence analysis likely assumes µ[i] is initialized close to J(θ), but this is not stated. In practice, a poor initialization could slow convergence. While common to assume zero initialization, the dependence on this choice should be discussed, especially since the average reward setting is sensitive to the baseline estimate. This makes the algorithm underspecified for reproducibility.

comments.title: Inconsistency in exponent notation across text
comments.explanation: Throughout the paper, exponents are written with brackets, e.g., ǫ[−][2] instead of ǫ^(-2), likely due to OCR errors. While the meaning is clear (e.g., 'O(N 2/ǫ2 log(N/ǫ))' means O(N²/ε² log(N/ε))), the inconsistent spacing—sometimes 'ǫ[−][2]', sometimes '[−][2]' without space—suggests potential misreads. However, given the surrounding context, the intended exponents are recoverable. For example, 'ǫ[−][1]' consistently means 1/ε. This appears to be a formatting artifact rather than an author error, so it should not be flagged as a real issue.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting consensus-based actor-critic algorithm for multi-agent RL with average reward, claiming finite-time convergence and sample complexity matching single-agent RL. While the algorithm design is novel and addresses important practical constraints (no joint action knowledge), the theoretical analysis contains critical errors that undermine the convergence guarantees. The step-size condition in Theorem 2 is mathematically impossible, and there are inconsistencies in the reported sample complexities. The experimental section is too brief to fully validate the theoretical claims.

comments.title: Impossible step-size condition in Theorem 2
comments.explanation: The first term in the minimum is problematic. Since η ∈ (0,1/N] for a doubly stochastic matrix (as noted in Assumption 3), we have η-N < 0 for N > 1. This makes 1/η - N/(η-N) negative, and thus the entire first term 2(1/η - N/(η-N))/(N-1) is negative. Since β must be positive for learning, this condition cannot be satisfied. This is a critical mathematical error that invalidates the convergence guarantee in Theorem 2.

comments.title: Inconsistent sample complexity reporting
comments.quote: Table 1 shows sample complexity O(N 2/ǫ2 log(N/ǫ)) for 'Thispaper', while Theorem 2 states critic complexity is O(N/ǫ log(N/ǫ))
comments.explanation: There's an inconsistency between Table 1 and the theorems. Theorem 2 states the critic has complexity O(N/ε log(N/ε)). Theorem 3 claims total complexity O(N²/ε² log(N/ε)). If the actor needs O(1/ε) iterations and each iteration uses the critic, the total should be O(N/ε² log(N/ε)), not O(N²/ε²). The extra factor of N is unexplained. This inconsistency makes it unclear what the true sample complexity is.

comments.title: Misleading communication complexity claim
comments.quote: the communication complexity is (Kc + tgossip)T = O(1/ǫ log(N/ǫ))... The amount of information (in terms of bits) is (KcNK + BNtgossip)T = O(N²/ǫ² log(N/ǫ))
comments.explanation: The paper claims communication complexity O(1/ε log(N/ε)) but then states the bit complexity is O(N²/ε² log(N/ε)). This is confusing and potentially misleading. The standard definition of communication complexity in distributed optimization refers to the total number of bits transmitted. By calling the rounds 'communication complexity' and bits 'amount of information', the paper may mislead readers about the actual communication cost.

comments.title: Overly restrictive assumption in Assumption 4
comments.quote: for any u ∈ R[K] , Φu ̸= 1, where 1 is an all-one vector
comments.explanation: This assumption states that the feature matrix Φ cannot map any vector to the all-ones vector. This is overly restrictive. The standard assumption in linear function approximation for average reward RL is that the all-ones vector is not in the column space of Φ, or equivalently, that there exists no u such that Φu = c*1 for any scalar c. As stated, the assumption is too strong and unnecessarily limits the class of admissible feature matrices.

comments.title: Undefined variable in Theorem 3
comments.explanation: The variable ρ appears in the batch size condition of Theorem 3 but is not defined in the visible text. While it might be defined elsewhere in the paper, its appearance here without definition makes the condition uninterpretable in isolation. This could confuse readers trying to understand the requirements for the batch size.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: This paper presents a theoretical analysis, including finite-time convergence and sample complexity, for a decentralized actor-critic reinforcement learning algorithm in an average-reward setting. The paper makes a significant contribution by extending these results to a multi-agent setting without requiring joint action observations, matching the state-of-the-art sample complexity of single-agent equivalents. While the theoretical framework is sound and the results are well-contextualized, there are minor inconsistencies between the stated notation and the formal mathematical expressions provided in the algorithms, likely arising from the transcription of the complex multi-agent indices.

comments.title: Notation in consensus weight updates
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, lines 11, the text defines the consensus update, but the summation indices and parameter symbols appear inconsistent. Specifically, it should conceptually be an update of the local weight using the neighbors' estimates, such as $w_{k+1}^{(i)} = \sum_{j \in N_i} [A]_{ij} \tilde{w}_k^{(j)}$. The provided text uses $\tilde{w}_k^{(i)}$ inside the sum, which ignores the consensus aspect with neighbors. However, the intent is clear given the context of Assumption 3 and the standard consensus literature mentioned in Section 3.2.

comments.title: Index inconsistency in Algorithm 2 input/output
comments.explanation: The OCR transcription of the matrix $\Delta_0$ has disrupted the display of indices and subscripts in the matrix definition. Specifically, the subscripts $t,0$ vs $t, B-1$ are conflated with the agent indices $1$ to $N$. While the intent of aggregating local TD-errors across agents and batches is mathematically standard for this class of distributed algorithms, the notation here is physically corrupted and would be difficult to parse without prior knowledge of this specific multi-agent consensus literature.

comments.title: Variable definition mismatch
comments.explanation: In the Actor Step (Algorithm 2, line 7), the update uses $\mu_{t,l+1}^{(i)}$, but the subsequent update of the TD-error in line 8 uses $\mu_{t,l}^{(i)}$. While typical in stochastic approximation variants, the paper does not explicitly state whether the TD-error update uses the *newly* updated average return estimate or the *previous* one. Given the context of standard temporal difference updates, this is a minor underspecification, but it could lead to confusion regarding the bias of the gradient estimate.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a decentralized multi-agent actor-critic algorithm for average-reward MARL and provides finite-time convergence and sample complexity analysis. The claims are ambitious and the results, if correct, would be a significant contribution. However, there are several technical issues that raise concerns about the validity of the proofs and the practical implementation. The most critical issues include a suspicious contraction factor that may not be less than one, the use of local average-reward estimates without consensus in the critic step (potentially introducing bias), and a garbled condition in Theorem 2 that is impossible to interpret. Additionally, key definitions (e.g., matrix A_πθ) are missing from the main text, making the assumptions difficult to verify. The experimental comparison is limited but adequate for a theory paper. Overall, the paper requires substantial clarification and correction before conclusions can be trusted.

comments.title: Suspicious contraction factor gamma in Theorem 2
comments.explanation: The contraction factor is defined as γ = (1−η N^{-1})·(1+2β). Since (1+2β) > 1 and (1−η N^{-1}) is close to 1 for large N (η may be as small as 1/N, making ηN^{-1} ≈ 1/N^2), the product can easily exceed 1, contradicting the claim that γ < 1. The condition on β that follows is garbled, so it is unclear whether the bound on β actually ensures γ < 1. This is a mathematical error that undermines the convergence proof of the critic.

comments.title: Local average-reward estimates in critic without consensus
comments.quote: Update µ[i] k,τ+1 ← (1−β)·µ[i] k,τ + β·r[i] k,τ+1; Update δk,τ[i] ← r[i] k,τ+1 − µ[i] k,τ + φ^T(s_{k,τ+1})w_k[i] − φ^T(s_{k,τ})w_k[i]
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, each agent maintains its own local estimate µ[i] of the average reward using its local rewards. These µ[i] are never communicated or averaged across agents. The TD-error δ[i] is then computed using this local µ[i]. The actor step later relies on an average of these local TD-errors to approximate the global advantage function. If the µ[i] estimates differ significantly (which they will, since each agent only sees its own reward stream), the average of the local TD-errors is not guaranteed to be an unbiased estimate of the global advantage. The paper does not analyze this bias or provide conditions under which it vanishes. This is a critical gap in both the algorithm design and the theoretical justification.

comments.title: Garbled condition on beta in Theorem 2
comments.explanation: The first term in the minimum is badly garbled, likely due to OCR. It appears to read "2(1η−N η−N−1 )" which is nonsensical as written. Even if we assume the intended form is something like "2(1-η)/(η N^{-1})" or similar, the meaning is unclear. Without a clear condition on β, the convergence guarantee of the critic is not properly stated. This obscures an important parameter regime and makes it impossible to verify the theoretical claims.

comments.title: Missing definition of matrix A_πθ
comments.quote: This property further implies that for all θ, Aπθ is invertible and λmax(Aπθ + A[T] πθ) < 0
comments.explanation: The matrix A_πθ is used throughout the assumptions and analysis (e.g., Assumptions 4 and 5) but is never explicitly defined in the main text. It is referenced as "(1)" but equation (1) is not included in the provided excerpt. Readers need to know its definition (presumably related to the TD fixed point) to understand the assumptions about its eigenvalues. This omission makes the theoretical setup incomplete.

comments.title: Potential ambiguity in the policy gradient theorem for MARL
comments.explanation: The policy gradient theorem in Equation (4) shows that the gradient of the global average reward with respect to agent i's parameter depends on the global advantage function A_θ(s,a). However, agent i only observes its own action a[i], not the full joint action a. The algorithm approximates the advantage using the average of local TD-errors. It is not explicitly proven that this approximation is unbiased or that the gradient direction remains approximately correct. The paper argues that the average of local TD-errors estimates the global advantage, but it relies on the assumption that the average of local µ[i] estimates equals the global average reward, which is not ensured without consensus on µ. This is closely related to the second issue above.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents a theoretically motivated approach to finite-time convergence in multi-agent actor-critic algorithms for average reward problems. The formulation is clear and the motivation addresses real-world constraints like decentralized information. However, the provided text (extracted via OCR) contains severe logical and mathematical inconsistencies in the core theoretical results (Theorems 2 and 3) and constraints (Assumption 3). Specifically, the sample complexity claims contradict the derived step-size requirements, and a stability constraint on the step-size is mathematically impossible for connected graphs. The dependency on $N$ also varies significantly between the Abstract/Table and the Theorem statements.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper presents a theoretically grounded finite-time convergence analysis for a fully decentralized actor-critic MARL algorithm in the average reward setting. The problem setup is well-motivated, and the consensus-based TD-sharing approach offers a plausible improvement in communication efficiency over prior work. However, the manuscript contains several significant mathematical and notational issues that impede verification of the core theoretical claims. Many critical bounds, step-size conditions, and matrix definitions are either missing, incorrectly formatted, or heavily garbled (likely due to PDF extraction), making it difficult to assess the correctness of the convergence rates. Additionally, there are minor inconsistencies in notation and parameter usage across sections. Addressing these clarifications and correcting the mathematical formulations is essential before the results can be fully accepted.

comments.title: Missing summation notation in Q-function definition
comments.explanation: The definition of the state-action value function appears to be missing the summation operator over time steps. In the average reward setting, $Q_	heta(s,a)$ is typically defined as the expected sum of differential rewards: $\mathbb{E}[\sum_{t=0}^\infty (\bar{r}_{t+1} - J(\theta)) \mid \dots]$. As written, the expression $E[\sum_{t=0}^\infty r_{\bar{t}+1} - J(\theta)]$ is ambiguous and mathematically malformed. The intended parenthetical structure around the summand is missing.

comments.title: Missing equation in Policy Gradient Theorem
comments.quote: Theorem 1 (Policy Gradient Theorem for MARL (Zhang et al., 2018)). For any θ, let πθ : S ×A → [0, 1] be a policy and let J(θ) be the global average long-term average return defined in (2). Then, the gradient of J(θ) with respect to parameter θ[i] can be computed as:
comments.explanation: The theorem statement ends with a colon but no subsequent equation is provided. The text later references 'the policy gradient rule in (4)', but Equation (4) is not displayed in the provided context. This omission breaks the logical flow and makes it impossible to verify how the decentralized gradient is formulated or how it connects to the advantage function used in the actor step.

comments.title: Unclear or garbled step-size and convergence conditions in Theorems 2 & 3
comments.explanation: Multiple critical parameter constraints in Theorems 2 and 3 are severely garbled, likely due to OCR/typesetting errors. The condition for the critic step-size $\beta$ contains an unreadable denominator. The contraction factor $\gamma$ for consensus dynamics is malformed. Most concerning, the actor step-size is stated as $\alpha = 4L_J$, which is dimensionally suspect and likely should be $\alpha = 4/L_J$ or similar. The bounds for batch size $B$ and gossip rounds $t_{gossip}$ are heavily corrupted, obscuring the dependence on problem constants ($\rho, \kappa, R_w, \epsilon$). These errors prevent verification of the finite-time convergence claims and reproducibility of the experimental settings.

comments.title: Indexing typo in Actor update equation
comments.explanation: Line 20 of Algorithm 2 and the subsequent equation show $\theta_t^i + 1 \leftarrow \theta_t^i + \dots$. The '+1' is misplaced on the LHS variable name. It should clearly be $\theta_{t+1}^i \leftarrow \theta_t^i + \dots$. This is a typographical error that could mislead readers about the update rule's temporal indexing.

comments.title: Incomplete feature assumption condition
comments.explanation: Assumption 4 states 'for any $u \in \mathbb{R}^K$, $\Phi u \neq 1$'. In average reward RL with linear function approximation, the standard assumption to ensure identifiability and prevent reward-shifting artifacts is that no non-zero feature vector lies in the span of the all-ones vector, i.e., $\Phi u \neq c \mathbf{1}$ for any scalar $c \in \mathbb{R}$. The stated condition appears to be missing the scalar multiplier and vector notation, which is a standard convention in the literature (e.g., Tsitsiklis & Van Roy, 1999).

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for average reward estimator
comments.quote: μt+1 = (1 − α) · μt + α · r̄t+1 is the estimate for the average long term return, and α is the step-size for the actor step.
comments.explanation: In Section 4, the authors use the actor step-size $\alpha$ to update the temporal average of the reward $\mu_t$, while the critic step uses a separate step-size $\beta$. While reusing a single step-size parameter is not forbidden, it introduces ambiguity in the two-timescale analysis. Typically, the reward estimator is updated with its own constant or the critic step-size to decouple the value estimation error from the policy gradient noise. Clarifying why $\alpha$ is chosen here and how it interacts with the $\epsilon$-accuracy requirements would strengthen the theoretical justification.

comments.title: Presentation inconsistency regarding network size N in complexity bounds
comments.quote: Our obtained complexity is O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )), where ǫ is the closeness to the neighborhood of stationary point (treating network size N as a fixed constant).
comments.explanation: The abstract and Table 1 explicitly state the sample complexity as $O(N^2/\epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon))$, while the contributions section and Theorem 3 text drop the $N$ dependence by treating it as a constant. While this is mathematically permissible, it creates a discrepancy in how the algorithm's scalability is presented. The table explicitly includes $N^2$, which suggests the authors are aware of and contribute to the $N$-dependence, yet the main text downplays it. Explicitly reconciling the big-O notation throughout would improve consistency.
